The surgeon frees any adhesions before inserting the trocars, then gently pushes away the cecum and ileum to inspect the appendix. The appendix is absent, indicating the patient has previously undergone an appendectomy.

The surgeon examines the adnexa and the Douglas pouch, as well as the anterior aspect of the uterus, noting the presence of a previous caesarean section scar.

The surgeon uses the Thunderbeat device to perform the first step of the hysterectomy, which involves incising the round ligament. The assistant holds the round ligament in position, allowing for coagulation and cutting.
The assistant positions the round ligament to expose the anterior leaf of the broad ligament. The surgeon then incises the anterior leaf, directing the incision toward the isthmus.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum, coagulating visible vessels one by one. A window is created in the broad ligament above the ureter, ensuring the ureter remains below. The assistant anteverts the uterus to maintain clear visualization of the ureter's position.
The surgeon decides to perform an inter-annexial procedure, coagulating and cutting to preserve the ovaries while removing the fallopian tubes to reduce the risk of ovarian cancer.

The surgeon coagulates and cuts along the limit of the fallopian tube, ensuring proximity to avoid devascularizing the ovary. The ovary is held in place while bipolar energy is used for efficient coagulation without excessive tension in the tissue.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field to ensure there is no bleeding. They proceed to dissect the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, carefully positioning the ureter laterally. The dissection is directed toward the root of the uterosacral ligament, with attention to the ureter's location.
The surgeon uses an instrument to push the ureter laterally, ensuring it is positioned correctly for dissection. The ureter is clearly visible as the surgeon carefully follows its path.

The surgeon dissects around the uterine vessels at the level of the isthmus, ensuring the ureter is safely positioned away from the coagulation site. Large vessels are carefully coagulated, and the same procedure is mirrored on the opposite side.

The surgeon coagulates and incises the round ligament while the uterus is in an intermediate position. The valve is pushed to visualize the vaginal cul-de-sac, with the surgeon's hands actively engaged in maintaining the position and visibility.
The surgeon dissects the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, progressing towards the uterine isthmus.

The surgeon identifies a thin leaf of tissue, marked by a black area, and confirms the ureter is positioned below. The fallopian tube is held, revealing an interruption due to a previous tubal ligation.

The surgeon holds the round ligament with a large grip and adjusts the bipolar instrument for optimal positioning. The ureter is safely displaced laterally by the valve, minimizing risk during the procedure.

The surgeon observes the disappearance of small bubbles in the tissue, indicating that water is present and the tissue is not fully coagulated. Once the bubbles disappear, the surgeon proceeds to cut the tissue.
The surgeon identifies the uterine artery and holds it in place. The bipolar forceps are opened to ensure hemostasis is achieved between the tissue layers.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to achieve hemostasis between two structures, then removes it. On the opposite side, the round ligament is held while Maria pushes the valve laterally to displace the ureter. Bleeding is observed from an opened vein, with the ureter visible nearby.

The surgeon identifies a vessel within the grasp of the surgical instrument, confirming the location of the ureter nearby. The visual focus is on ensuring the vessel and ureter are properly positioned and visible.

The surgeon identifies a vein and uses a valve to push away the ureter. The uterine pedicles are controlled at the isthmus, preparing to cut and devascularize the uterus. Some residual bleeding from the uterus is visible, but no active bleeding from circulation.

The uterine artery is visible, and the surgeon notes the change in color of the uterus as it becomes devascularized. The uterine pedicles are cut, leading to a cessation of bleeding as the uterus is repositioned to the middle.

Maria pushes the valve anteriorly to dissect the bladder, ensuring no bleeding occurs. The surgeon then incises the cervix and proceeds to incise the vagina, maintaining contact with the cervix throughout the procedure.
The surgeon uses a valve to push the bladder, revealing its location and ensuring a clear edge of the vagina for closure. The pneumo gas is visible as it expands the surgical field.

The surgeon opens the vaginal wall using a monopolar hook or energy device, creating an incision in close contact with the cervix. The third operator assists by turning the valve counterclockwise to facilitate the dissection.
On the left side of the patient, the surgeon opens the vagina, resulting in a loss of pneumoperitoneum. The assistant is instructed to take hold of the round ligament.

The surgeon holds the round ligament on the left side of the patient. Once the vagina is opened, the third operator is instructed to gently push, ensuring not to apply excessive force to prevent tearing of the vaginal tissue.

The surgeon carefully exposes the surgical field, reducing the strength of pushing to avoid contact with the bowel while using a monopolar hook. The assistant holds the endoscope steady to ensure clear visualization.

The surgeon positions the valve in front of the uterus, lowering it to access the right cul de sac. The incision is made over the right cul de sac, maintaining close contact with the cervix while avoiding lateral incision from previous coagulation. The valve is used to separate and increase the distance between the surgical instruments and the ureter, which is pushed laterally.

The surgeon completes the final centimeters of dissection, ensuring minimal pressure on the uterus for exposure. The assistant gently removes the uterus through the vaginal opening, completing the hysterectomy procedure.
